content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 15, 1977

George Kading
County Counsel
Santa Barbara, California 93102

Dear Mr. Kading:

Thank you for your letter in which you ask whether a
designated employee under a local conflict of interest
code must report his interest in a trust if the employee
named as the beneficiary will not receive any benefits
until the death of another beneficiary. My apologies
for not answering your letter sooner.

The Commission has received a letter by Senator
Garamendi on the subject of reporting trusts. At a
recent meeting, the Commission directed the staff to
review this area and recommend either regulations or
legislative amendments which would clarify the reporting
requirements. Your letter provides us with another
relevant factual situation which will be useful in our
deliberations. Pending the Commission review, we believe
that there should be no disclosure by your employee.

Please call me if you have any questions regarding
this letter.

Sincerely,

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

RMS:nc

Enclosure

GEORGE P. KADING
County Counsel

OFFICE OF THE

COUNTY COUNSEL
SANTA BARBARA COUNTY

DEPUTIES
Susan Trescher
Melbourne B. Wedd
Marvin Levine
Don H. Vickers
William R. Allen
C. William Allman
Paula Kimbrell

105 E. Anapamu St.
Santa Barbara, Calif. 93101
Telephone: 966-1611

ROBERT D. CHWIEL
Chief Assistant

DANA D. SMITH
Assistant

May 18, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

We seek your opinion on whether a designated employee under a
local Conflict of Interest Code must report his interest in a
trust or report the interests held by a trust where the employee is named as a beneficiary but will receive no income
until the death of some other beneficiary. If interests are
reportable in certain circumstances what effect, if any, would
certain possible variations in the terms of the trust have?
Possible variations which we have in mind include whether or
not the trustee has the power to invade principal for the benefit of the current beneficiary and whether or not the employee
may determine who will benefit from the trust in the event he
does not survive the current beneficiary.

Our question assumes that the trust and the assets of the trust
would be otherwise reportable by the employee if they were held
directly by him or by a business entity in which the employee
has an ownership interest.

We enclose for your information a copy of a letter prepared by
us on the subject; however, our question is more general than
the exact situation discussed therein.

Thank you for your assistance in the matter. If further information or clarification is needed please contact this office.

Very truly yours,

GEORGE P. KADING, COUNTY COUNSEL

GPK:hh
